metadata.source_inspiration: Charlie Munger, Daniel Kahneman, Naval Ravikant, Shane Parrish

models.name: Reward Superresponse Tendency
models.description: People respond extremely strongly to incentives and rewards
models.example: Salespeople gaming commission structures

models.name: Liking/Loving Tendency
models.description: We distort reality to fit what we like or love
models.example: Ignoring faults in favorite products or people

models.name: Disliking/Hating Tendency
models.description: We distort reality to fit what we dislike
models.example: Dismissing ideas from rival teams automatically

models.name: Doubt-Avoidance Tendency
models.description: People rush to remove doubt by making quick decisions
models.example: Buying on impulse to end decision anxiety

models.name: Inconsistency-Avoidance Tendency
models.description: People resist changing ideas once committed
models.example: Continuing failing projects because of sunk investment

models.name: Curiosity Tendency
models.description: Humans are naturally curious, which can be harnessed
models.example: Engaging learning through questions not lectures

models.name: Kantian Fairness Tendency
models.description: People seek fairness and reciprocity in interactions
models.example: Successful negotiations require both parties to benefit

models.name: Envy/Jealousy Tendency
models.description: People are driven by envy more than greed
models.example: Employees more upset by peer raises than market rates

models.name: Reciprocation Tendency
models.description: People feel obligated to return favors
models.example: Free samples leading to purchases

models.name: Influence-from-Mere-Association
models.description: We judge by associations, not just merit
models.example: Premium pricing creating perception of quality

models.name: Pain-Avoiding Psychological Denial
models.description: People deny painful realities
models.example: Ignoring health symptoms or relationship problems

models.name: Excessive Self-Regard Tendency
models.description: People overestimate their own abilities and decisions
models.example: 90% of drivers think they're above average

models.name: Overoptimism Tendency
models.description: People systematically overestimate positive outcomes
models.example: Projects consistently take 2x expected time

models.name: Deprival Superreaction
models.description: Losses feel ~2x as bad as equivalent gains feel good
models.example: Fighting harder to keep $100 than to gain $100

models.name: Social Proof Tendency
models.description: People follow what others are doing
models.example: Restaurant lines, market bubbles, bystander effect

models.name: Contrast-Misreaction
models.description: We judge things by contrast, not absolute value
models.example: $500 discount on $20k car feels bigger than on $1k laptop

models.name: Stress-Influence Tendency
models.description: Stress triggers extreme responses
models.example: Panic selling in market crashes

models.name: Availability-Misweighing
models.description: Vivid, recent events seem more probable
models.example: Fearing plane crashes more than car crashes

models.name: Use-It-or-Lose-It
models.description: Skills and knowledge atrophy without practice
models.example: Language fluency fading without use

models.name: Authority-Misinfluence
models.description: People follow authority figures blindly
models.example: Milgram experiment, experts quoted out of context

models.name: Twaddle Tendency
models.description: People prefer complex explanations over simple ones
models.example: Overcomplicating simple business strategies

models.name: Reason-Respecting Tendency
models.description: People respond better when given reasons
models.example: 'Because' increases compliance dramatically

models.name: Lollapalooza Tendency
models.description: Multiple psychological biases reinforcing create extreme outcomes
models.example: Market bubbles, cult formation, mass hysteria

models.name: Bayesian Updating
models.description: Update beliefs as new evidence arrives
models.example: Revising project timelines as new data comes in

models.name: Compounding
models.description: Small gains accumulate exponentially over time
models.example: Knowledge, relationships, investments grow exponentially

models.name: Expected Value
models.description: Multiply probability by outcome to compare options
models.example: Insurance decisions, investment analysis

models.name: Base Rates
models.description: Start with the statistical average, then adjust
models.example: Most startups fail; most predictions are wrong

models.name: Normal Distribution
models.description: Many phenomena cluster around average with decreasing tails
models.example: Height, test scores, quality control

models.name: Power Laws
models.description: Small number of causes produce majority of effects
models.example: 80/20 rule: 20% of customers = 80% of revenue

models.name: Regression to Mean
models.description: Extreme outcomes tend toward average over time
models.example: One-hit wonders, sports slumps, luck vs skill

models.name: Margin of Safety
models.description: Build in buffers for inevitable errors
models.example: Engineering, investing, planning with headroom

models.name: Optionality
models.description: Prefer choices that preserve future options
models.example: Cash reserves, versatile skills, reversible decisions

models.name: Feedback Loops
models.description: Circular cause-effect chains amplify or stabilize
models.example: virtuous/vicious cycles, thermostat systems

models.name: Emergence
models.description: Complex behaviors arise from simple interactions
models.example: Ant colonies, markets, consciousness from neurons

models.name: Critical Mass/Phase Transition
models.description: Small changes tip systems past threshold
models.example: Nuclear chain reactions, viral adoption, habit formation

models.name: Entropy
models.description: Systems naturally move toward disorder without energy input
models.example: Companies stagnating without innovation, relationships need work

models.name: Inertia
models.description: Objects (and people) resist changes to motion
models.example: Market incumbents, established habits, organizational culture

models.name: Activation Energy
models.description: Small push can start large chain reactions
models.example: Getting started is hardest part; catalysts in chemistry and life

models.name: Incentive Superresponse
models.description: People respond to incentives in extreme, unexpected ways
models.example: Wells Fargo fake accounts, teaching to the test

models.name: Comparative Advantage
models.description: Trade benefits all parties even if one is better at everything
models.example: Specialization, outsourcing, team roles

models.name: Creative Destruction
models.description: Innovation destroys old industries while creating new ones
models.example: Netflix vs Blockbuster, smartphones vs cameras

models.name: Network Effects
models.description: Product value increases with more users
models.example: Social media, marketplaces, languages, standards

models.name: Switching Costs
models.description: Costs (real or perceived) of changing products
models.example: Phone ecosystems, professional software, relationships

models.name: Economies of Scale
models.description: Per-unit costs decrease with volume
models.example: Manufacturing, cloud computing, content creation

models.name: Tragedy of the Commons
models.description: Individuals acting rationally destroy shared resources
models.example: Overfishing, pollution, office refrigerator

models.name: Prisoner's Dilemma
models.description: Individual rationality leads to collective bad outcomes
models.example: Price wars, arms races, environmental agreements

models.name: Inversion
models.description: Think backward from failure instead of forward from success
models.example: What would guarantee project failure? Avoid those things.

models.name: Circle of Competence
models.description: Know your boundaries; invest only where you have edge
models.example: Buffett avoiding tech, experts staying in their domain

models.name: Second-Order Thinking
models.description: Ask 'and then what?' repeatedly
models.example: Price cut → more sales → competitor response → price war

models.name: Opportunity Cost
models.description: The true cost is what you give up
models.example: Time spent on X can't be spent on Y; capital allocation

models.name: Decision Trees
models.description: Map all branches and probabilities visually
models.example: Strategic planning, litigation, major investments

models.name: Pre-Mortem Analysis
models.description: Imagine future failure, work backward to prevent it
models.example: Projects, launches, relationships - 'it's 2027 and we failed'

models.name: Scenario Planning
models.description: Prepare for multiple possible futures
models.example: Best case, worst case, most likely case preparation

models.name: Steel-Manning
models.description: Strengthen opponent's argument before refuting it
models.example: Better decisions, better relationships, intellectual honesty

models.name: OODA Loop
models.description: Observe-Orient-Decide-Act cycle for rapid decisions
models.example: Military, trading, emergency response

models.name: Red Teaming
models.description: Assign someone to argue against the group's position
models.example: NASA, intelligence agencies, board decisions

models.name: Checklist Manifesto
models.description: Use checklists for complex, repeatable processes
models.example: Surgery, aviation, investing (Munger's psychological checklist)

models.name: Natural Selection
models.description: Traits that aid survival/reproduction spread
models.example: Market competition, meme spread, organizational culture

models.name: Adaptation
models.description: Organisms adapt to environmental pressures over time
models.example: Career pivots, business model changes, skill development

models.name: Niche Specialization
models.description: Specialists outperform generalists in stable environments
models.example: Domain experts, luxury brands, focused startups
